FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data (Release 5.1); N=495 adolescents with EARS app data at Year-4 follow-up.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures SPECIFIC smartphone variables: passive smartphone tracking via EARS app (social media use in hours), self-reported social media time, and video game addiction (VGAQ). Also measures SMAQ (social media addiction scale).

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests linking smartphone/SM variables to non-SM outcomes. Linear mixed models with family as random effect; logistic regression models. Examples: social media (EARS) correlated with externalizing symptoms (β=0.07, 95% CI 0.03-0.12); self-reported social media correlated with internalizing symptoms (β=0.05, 95% CI 0.01-0.09).

FT_IC4.evidence: Extractable numerics reported: β coefficients, OR values, 95% CIs, p-values throughout. Examples: EARS social media→externalizing (β=0.07, 95% CI 0.03-0.12, p=0.002); self-report→SMAQ (β=0.15, 95% CI 0.10-0.20).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Compares self-reported and objective (EARS) smartphone/SM measures and analyzes associations with behavioral outcomes and addiction scales.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (npj Mental Health Research, 2025). Not a preprint, duplicate, or deposit.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Paper reports smartphone/SM as INDEPENDENT variable predicting behavioral outcomes (externalizing, internalizing) and addiction measures, NOT as dependent variable. Also includes cross-sectional and individual psychological predictors (picture vocabulary predicts reporting bias).
notes: E5: Measurement paper (compares self-report vs objective EARS measures) that ALSO reports SM↔non-SM associations, so FT-IC5 MET. E8: Paper reports inferential stats (linear mixed models, logistic regression with reported coefficients and p-values), not just descriptive means/SDs.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; N=9465 children (aged 9-11 years at baseline) followed 2016-2021.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures 'average daily screen time' as composite variable with no phone/SM breakdown. Abstract states 'screen time' but does not specify smartphone, social media, or other qualifying modalities. Composite screen time without SM-specific coefficient is exclusionary per rule.

FT_IC3.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract indicates prospective association between screen time and BED (OR=1.09, 95% CI [1.03, 1.14]) and mediation by depression, but cannot verify full analysis without PDF.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract reports OR and CI values, but cannot verify extractability or completeness without document.

FT_IC5.evidence: No PDF available. Abstract suggests empirical analysis (generalized SEM mediation analysis), but full methodology cannot be confirmed.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (International Journal of Eating Disorders, 2024). Not identified as duplicate/preprint.

FT_EC7_reason: Excluded at FT-IC2 (composite screen time without phone/SM breakdown). FT-EC7 irrelevant.
notes: EXCLUSION: FT-IC2 NOT_MET. Paper measures generic 'average daily screen time' composite without smartphone-specific or social media-specific breakdown. Per screening criteria, generic 'total screen time' STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data; prospective cohort N=10,757 children (ages 10-14 years) baseline to Year 2 follow-up, 2016-2020.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures SPECIFIC screen time modalities including: television/streaming, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chat, social media (Facebook, Instagram). These are distinct smartphone/SM variables per FT-IC2 definition.

FT_IC3.evidence: Reports inferential statistical tests: mixed-effect models with robust standard errors. Examples: parental monitoring associated with less total screen time (B=-0.37, 95% CI -0.58, -0.16); family conflict associated with more YouTube videos (B=0.03, 95% CI 0.01, 0.04); video games (B=0.02, 95% CI 0.0004, 0.04); television (B=0.02, 95% CI 0.003, 0.03).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple extractable numerics: B coefficients and 95% CIs reported in Table 2 for all screen time associations with parental monitoring and family conflict across multiple outcomes and years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Prospective analysis examining associations between baseline parental monitoring/family conflict and repeated screen time measures at Year 1 and Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Acta Paediatrica, 2024). Not a preprint, duplicate, or deposit.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Screen time (YouTube, video games, television, etc.) is the DEPENDENT/OUTCOME variable, but PREDICTORS include family parenting factors (parental monitoring, family conflict), which are contextual/demographic-type variables. However, this is NOT purely demographic predictor (not just SES/race/age/income/education). Even if it were, paper also analyzes multiple screen time modalities cross-sectionally, which would exclude FT-EC7 applicability.
notes: E14: Parental monitoring (sociodemographic-type predictor) of screen time in prospective design, but analysis is across multiple screen modalities with specific coefficients. E15: Family conflict/parenting predictor of screen time in prospective design with specific coefficients.
